does: This paper develops methods to understand and control latent reasoning in Large Language Models that perform multi-step inference within hidden continuous states rather than generating explicit reasoning chains. The authors employ structural, causal, and geometric probes to systematically analyze how reasoning steps are encoded in latent vectors, discovering that early vectors function as critical causal hubs and contain compressed faithful representations of intermediate reasoning. They then translate these interpretability findings into a collection of training-free, decode-time interventions that manipulate latent vectors by imposing identified geometric and semantic constraints. Extensive experiments across different model scales and diverse reasoning tasks demonstrate that these interventions consistently improve reasoning accuracy without requiring retraining.
connects: This work bridges mechanistic interpretability and model control, extending the recent line of research on understanding hidden representations in LLMs through probing methods. It builds on prior work analyzing how neural networks encode conceptual information and how causal interventions can identify critical computational hubs, while addressing a specific gap around latent reasoning—the efficiency advantage of continuous-state inference over explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning. The paper responds to concerns about the opacity and controllability of hidden reasoning processes, connecting to broader efforts to make LLM internals more transparent and steerable. It combines insights from interpretability research with intervention-based control strategies similar to those used in other model modification work.
enables: This work opens pathways for more reliable and controllable implicit reasoning in LLMs. By establishing a direct link between interpretability and actionable control, it enables future development of reasoning-specific intervention techniques that could improve model reliability without expensive retraining. The geometric and semantic priors identified could guide design of more efficient inference methods or better prompting strategies. The approach suggests that latent reasoning systems can be systematically debugged and improved through targeted manipulation of key computational hubs, potentially leading to safer and more interpretable AI systems. Future work could extend these interventions to other reasoning domains, explore their interaction with scaling, and investigate whether similar patterns hold across different model architectures.
